turns.user_message: I noticed that I automatically apologize for completely normal things, like taking up space in a doorway or asking a simple question. It just slips out before I can stop it, and I feel this heavy little knot in my chest every time. I know it’s ridiculous to feel guilty for existing, but the habit is so ingrained now. I just wish I could go through a whole afternoon without feeling like I’m constantly inconveniencing everyone around me.

I keep noticing how I rehearse apologies in my head before I even step into a room. I shrink myself down to make sure I don’t bother anyone, and it leaves me completely drained. Every little misstep feels like proof that I’m fundamentally flawed, and I just carry that weight everywhere I go. I try to catch myself, but the shame just loops back around, making me feel like I’m taking up too much air.

I’ve started noticing that my very presence feels like a mistake I can never quite make up for. I catch myself holding my breath whenever I speak, terrified that any wrong word will confirm how deeply I’m messing everything up. It feels like I’m permanently stained by my own existence, and no amount of quieting down will ever erase it. I just sit with this heavy, sinking certainty that I’m a burden, and I don’t know how to shake it off.

generation_metadata.diversity_anchor: The speaker opens with something they noticed about themselves.
